text: A central challenge in the emerging field of molecular spintronics is the identification of spin qubit materials capable of operating under practical conditions for quantum information applications. Recent studies have highlighted photoexcited chromophore–radical systems as promising candidates for expanding the current library of molecular qubits. In this work, we use transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) techniques to investigate a series of three chromophore–radical compounds consisting of a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore linked to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. By exploring the effects of isotope labeling on the TEMPO moiety, we analyze changes in the EPR spectra and electron spin coherence times ($T_m$) of the molecular quartet states generated upon photoexcitation. Our findings demonstrate that embedding these molecules in a rigid polymer matrix enables the coherent manipulation of spin states even at room temperature, reaching a spin coherence time of 0.7 $\mu$s. This observation of ambient-temperature electron spin coherence marks a significant step toward integrating functional molecular spin systems into practical quantum devices.
